text: Hi friends, this video is a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with intraoperative cholangiogram. This is a forty five year old gentleman who presented with obstructive jaundice secondary to common bile duct stones.

There was a small eight millimeter stone which was reported in MRCP. Patient was planned for ERCP but he had a pyloric stenosis which in spite of a couple of attempts to do dilatation the ERCP scope could not be passed beyond. Only a UGI scopy could be done entering into the duodenum. There was no ulceration or growth in the level of the duodenum.

So then he was referred to us for laparoscopic cholecystectomy along with the lap CBD exploitation. So here you can see he had some form of peritonitis probably secondary to the dilatation which was attempted endoscopically.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to remove flimsy adhesions, creating a small window that reveals the gallbladder.

The surgeon encounters dense adhesions likely due to previous cholangitis and cholecystitis. Sharp dissection is used to carefully identify the correct anatomical plane, with monopolar energy applied in short bursts at low wattage.
The surgeon uses short bursts of monopolar energy to carefully release adhesions while staying close to the gallbladder, ensuring that the colon and duodenum remain outside the dissection area.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to expose the entire gallbladder, meticulously releasing adhesions between the lateral border of the gallbladder, the liver bed, and the gastrocolic omentum. The gallbladder is now fully visible.

The surgeon continues dissection to expand the area around the thick-walled and edematous gallbladder, aiming to expose the common bile duct. Fibrous strands are divided sharply, while remaining adhesions are moved away using blunt dissection.

The surgeon encounters thick adhesions and divides them sharply using either cold scissors or low voltage monopolar energy. The procedure is performed carefully to avoid thermal damage to vital structures, ensuring safe dissection without relying solely on ultrasonic devices.
The surgeon applies traction to identify the vascular plane before using monopolar energy in short bursts to dissect flimsy adhesions between the lower border of the liver.

The surgeon exposes the entire gallbladder and hepatoduodenal ligament area, retracting the duodenum downward. A small incision is made on the fat covering the hepatoduodenal ligament, revealing a vein. Careful dissection continues to expose important structures, ensuring tissues are hooked and pulled away from preserved structures before using energy.

The surgeon uses suction to perform blunt dissection, revealing the artery and vein clearly within the surgical field. A gauze is placed on the side to keep the field dry, aiding in the preservation of normal anatomy and important structures.

The surgeon carefully dissects the incision further cranially between the gallbladder and omental adhesions, ensuring the incision remains on the peritoneum to avoid deeper structures such as the common hepatic duct and hepatic artery. A suction device is used to maintain the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon identifies the entire gallbladder on the lateral aspect, applying traction to find the correct dissection plane. Both blunt and sharp dissection techniques are used to meticulously separate the posterior surface of the gallbladder, ensuring the dissection stays close to the gallbladder to avoid damaging nearby portal structures.

The gallbladder is being slowly dissected off the liver bed. After completing some posterior dissection, the surgeon creates an anterior window by incising the peritoneum, hooking it, and applying energy. An instrument is passed through the small window, which is then gradually expanded to facilitate further dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon encounters difficulty dissecting Calot's triangle and employs a safe technique to ensure thorough dissection around the suspected cystic duct and common bile duct, both anteriorly and posteriorly. The gallbladder is then dissected off the liver bed from both sides to open up a window, revealing the critical view of safety.

The surgeon demonstrates the cystic duct and the common bile duct (CBD) using an instrument, highlighting the window created between the liver bed and the cystic duct, with no other structures visible in this area.

The surgeon uses an instrument to milk the cystic duct, ensuring no remnant stones are present. The cystic duct appears wide, suggesting previous stone passage into the common bile duct. A proline suture is applied for intracorporeal ligation at the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct, followed by incision of the cystic duct using scissors.

Obstructed bile flows freely from the cystic duct, which is suctioned out. A small infant feeding tube is inserted, and saline is injected under pressure, causing fluid to gush out and clear any sludge. The tube is further advanced to check duct patency, and contrast dye is injected to visualize the intrahepatic ducts, common bile duct, and duodenum spill using fluoroscopy.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative cholangiogram, confirming the absence of remnant stones and washing away any sludge. The distal stump of the cystic duct is ligated using an endo loop. The gallbladder is carefully dissected from the liver bed, with attention to maintaining the correct tissue planes to avoid penetrating the liver parenchyma.

The surgeon positions the C-arm for imaging and inserts a catheter, such as an infant feeding tube, into the cystic duct to perform an intraoperative cholangiogram. A dermia basket is used through the cystic duct incision to retrieve any small stones from the common bile duct, demonstrating a transcystic approach.
The surgeon removes the entire gallbladder and places it into an endo bag. Sutures are applied in a pursing fashion to close the bag securely around the specimen.

The surgeon holds the gallbladder at the cystic duct region and places it into the endobag with the cystic duct portion towards the bag's opening. An incision is made to remove bile or stones as necessary. A wide drain is placed sub-hepatically to prevent post-operative leaks.
